subject: Chemistry
q: An organic compound " P " of molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ gives positive Iodoform test but negative Tollen's test. When " P " is treated with dilute acid, it produces " Q ". " Q " gives positive Tollen's test and also iodoform test. The structure of " P " is :
question: An organic compound " P " of molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ gives positive Iodoform test but negative Tollen's test. When " P " is treated with dilute acid, it produces " Q ". " Q " gives positive Tollen's test and also iodoform test. The structure of " P " is :
solution:
P gives positive iodoform (has $CH_3CO-$) but negative Tollen's (no $-CHO$).
Q (after acid hydrolysis) gives both tests, meaning Q has both $CH_3CO-$ and $-CHO$.
This indicates P has an acetal protecting an aldehyde group.
Option (1): $CH_3-CO-CH_2-CH(OCH_3)_2$
Has $CH_3CO-$ → positive iodoform
Has acetal $-CH(OCH_3)_2$, no free aldehyde → negative Tollen's
Hydrolysis: $-CH(OCH_3)_2 \xrightarrow{H^+/H_2O} -CHO$
Q = $CH_3-CO-CH_2-CHO$ gives both iodoform and Tollen's test
Molecular formula: $C_6H_{12}O_3$

subject: Chemistry
q:
A hydrocarbon ' $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{8}\right)$ on reaction with HCl gives an optically active compound ' Q ' $\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{Cl}\right)$ which on reaction with one mole of ammonia gives compound ' $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{~N}\right)$. ' $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}$ on diazotization followed by hydrolysis gives ' S '.
Identify $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{R}$ and S.
question:
A hydrocarbon ' $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{8}\right)$ on reaction with HCl gives an optically active compound ' Q ' $\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{Cl}\right)$ which on reaction with one mole of ammonia gives compound ' $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{~N}\right)$. ' $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}$ on diazotization followed by hydrolysis gives ' S '.
Identify $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{R}$ and S.
solution:
For Q ($C_4H_9Cl$) to be optically active, it must have a chiral center.
P ($C_4H_8$) = but-2-ene ($CH_3-CH=CH-CH_3$)
P + HCl → Q: $CH_3-CH=CH-CH_3 + HCl \rightarrow CH_3-CH(Cl)-CH_2-CH_3$ (2-chlorobutane, chiral at C-2)
Q + $NH_3$ → R: 2-chlorobutane + $NH_3 \rightarrow CH_3-CH(NH_2)-CH_2-CH_3$ (2-aminobutane)
R → diazotization → hydrolysis → S: 2-aminobutane $\xrightarrow{NaNO_2/HCl}$ diazonium salt $\xrightarrow{H_2O}$ $CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_2-CH_3$ (butan-2-ol)

subject: Chemistry
q: An organic compound $(\mathrm{P})$ on treatment with aqueous ammonia under hot condition forms compound (Q) which on heating with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ and KOH forms compound (R) having molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{~N}$. Names of P, Q and R respectively are.
question: An organic compound $(\mathrm{P})$ on treatment with aqueous ammonia under hot condition forms compound (Q) which on heating with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ and KOH forms compound (R) having molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{~N}$. Names of P, Q and R respectively are.
solution:
Q + $Br_2$/KOH → R is Hofmann bromamide degradation: Amide → Amine (with one less carbon).
R = $C_6H_7N$ = $C_6H_5NH_2$ = Aniline
For Q to give aniline, Q = Benzamide ($C_6H_5CONH_2$)
$C_6H_5CONH_2 \xrightarrow{Br_2/KOH} C_6H_5NH_2$
P + hot $NH_3$ → Q: Carboxylic acid + $NH_3$ → Amide
P = Benzoic acid ($C_6H_5COOH$)
$C_6H_5COOH \xrightarrow{NH_3, \Delta} C_6H_5CONH_2$
P = Benzoic acid, Q = Benzamide, R = Aniline

question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : The number of species among $\mathrm{SF}_{4}, \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+},\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-}, \mathrm{XeF}_{4},\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_{4}\right]^{2-}, \mathrm{SeF}_{4}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$, that have tetrahedral geometry is 3.
Statement II : In the set [$\mathrm{NO}_{2}, \mathrm{BeH}_{2}, \mathrm{BF}_{3}, \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$], all the molecules have incomplete octet around central atom.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
Statement I: Checking geometry of each species:
$SF_4$: 4 bp + 1 lp → see-saw
$NH_4^+$: 4 bp → tetrahedral
$[NiCl_4]^{2-}$: $Ni^{2+}$ (d⁸) with weak field $Cl^-$ → $sp^3$ → tetrahedral
$XeF_4$: 4 bp + 2 lp → square planar
$[PtCl_4]^{2-}$: $Pt^{2+}$ (d⁸) → $dsp^2$ → square planar
$SeF_4$: 4 bp + 1 lp → see-saw
$[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}$: $Ni^{2+}$ with strong field $CN^-$ → $dsp^2$ → square planar
Only 2 species are tetrahedral. Statement I is false.
Statement II: $NO_2$ (odd electron), $BeH_2$ (4e⁻), $BF_3$ (6e⁻), $AlCl_3$ (6e⁻) all have incomplete octet. Statement II is true.

q:
For the reaction, $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$, graph is plotted as shown below. Identify correct statements.
A. Standard free energy change for the reaction is $-5.40 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
B. As $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\ominus}$ in graph is positive, $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ will not dissociate into $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ at all.
C. Reverse reaction will go to completion.
D. When 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ changes into equilibrium mixture, value of $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\ominus}=-0.84 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
E. When 2 mole of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ changes into equilibrium mixture, $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\ominus}$ for equilibrium mixture is $-6.24 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
For the reaction, $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$, graph is plotted as shown below. Identify correct statements.
A. Standard free energy change for the reaction is $-5.40 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
B. As $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\ominus}$ in graph is positive, $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ will not dissociate into $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ at all.
C. Reverse reaction will go to completion.
D. When 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ changes into equilibrium mixture, value of $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\ominus}=-0.84 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
E. When 2 mole of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ changes into equilibrium mixture, $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\ominus}$ for equilibrium mixture is $-6.24 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
From the graph of G vs fraction of $N_2O_4$ dissociated:
At $x = 0$ (pure $N_2O_4$): $G = 0$
At equilibrium ($x \approx 0.4$): $G = -0.84$ kJ/mol (minimum)
At $x = 1$ (complete dissociation): $G = 5.40$ kJ/mol
Statement A: $\Delta G° = 5.40$ kJ/mol (positive), not $-5.40$ kJ/mol. False.
Statement B: Even with positive $\Delta G°$, equilibrium is established with partial dissociation. False.
Statement C: Reverse reaction reaches equilibrium, not completion. False.
Statement D: $\Delta G = G_{eq} - G_{N_2O_4} = -0.84 - 0 = -0.84$ kJ/mol. True.
Statement E: $\Delta G = G_{eq} - G_{NO_2} = -0.84 - 5.40 = -6.24$ kJ/mol. True.

q: Pre-exponential factors of two different reactions of same order are identical. Let activation energy of first reaction exceeds the activation energy of second reaction by $20 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. If $\mathrm{k}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ are the rate constants of first and second reaction respectively at 300 K, then $\ln \frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}$ will be $\_\_\_\_$. (nearest integer) $\left[\mathrm{R}=8.3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
question: Pre-exponential factors of two different reactions of same order are identical. Let activation energy of first reaction exceeds the activation energy of second reaction by $20 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. If $\mathrm{k}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ are the rate constants of first and second reaction respectively at 300 K, then $\ln \frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}$ will be $\_\_\_\_$. (nearest integer) $\left[\mathrm{R}=8.3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
solution:
Given: $A_1 = A_2$, $E_{a1} = E_{a2} + 20$ kJ/mol, T = 300 K, R = 8.3 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹
Using Arrhenius equation: $k = Ae^{-E_a/RT}$
$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{e^{-E_{a2}/RT}}{e^{-E_{a1}/RT}} = e^{(E_{a1} - E_{a2})/RT}$
$E_{a1} - E_{a2} = 20$ kJ/mol = 20000 J/mol
$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\frac{20000}{8.3 \times 300}} = e^{\frac{20000}{2490}}$
$\ln\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{20000}{2490} = 8.03 \approx 8$

Which of the following represents the correct trend for the mentioned property ?
A. $\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{P}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{B}$ $\ \ \ $ - $\ \ \ $ First Ionization Energy
B. $\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{P}$ $\ \ \ $ - $\ \ \ $ Electron Affinity
C. $\mathrm{K}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{B}$ $\ \ \ $ - $\ \ \ $ Metallic character
D. $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{MgO}>\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \quad$ $\ \ \ $ - $\ \ \ $ Basic character
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Which of the following represents the correct trend for the mentioned property ?
A. $\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{P}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{B}$ $\ \ \ $ - $\ \ \ $ First Ionization Energy
B. $\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{P}$ $\ \ \ $ - $\ \ \ $ Electron Affinity
C. $\mathrm{K}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{B}$ $\ \ \ $ - $\ \ \ $ Metallic character
D. $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{MgO}>\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \quad$ $\ \ \ $ - $\ \ \ $ Basic character
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
A. F > P > S > B (IE₁): F has highest IE₁, P > S due to half-filled $3p^3$ stability, B has lowest. Correct
B. Cl > F > S > P (EA): Cl has highest EA (F smaller, more repulsion), S > P (half-filled p-orbital). Correct
C. K > Al > Mg > B (Metallic character): Incorrect. Mg (Group 2) is more metallic than Al (Group 13) in same period. Correct order: K > Mg > Al > B
D. $K_2O > Na_2O > MgO > Al_2O_3$ (Basic character): Follows electropositive nature. $Al_2O_3$ is amphoteric. Correct
Correct statements: A, B and D only.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below : Statement I is true but Statement II is false Both Statement I and Statement II are false
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : Among $\left.\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4}\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{en})_{3}\right)\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$, $\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$ has the maximum number of unpaired electrons.
Statement II : The number of pairs among $\left\{\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-},\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}\right]\right\},\left\{\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-},\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}\right\}$ and $\left\{\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}\right],\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}\right\}$ that contain only diamagnetic species is two.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below : Statement I is true but Statement II is false Both Statement I and Statement II are false
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I: Unpaired electrons in each complex:
$[Cu(NH_3)_4]^{2+}$: $Cu^{2+}$ is $d^9$ → 1 unpaired
$[Ni(en)_3]^{2+}$: $Ni^{2+}$ is $d^8$, octahedral → 2 unpaired
$[Ni(NH_3)_6]^{2+}$: $Ni^{2+}$ is $d^8$ → 2 unpaired
$[Mn(H_2O)_6]^{2+}$: $Mn^{2+}$ is $d^5$, weak field → high spin → 5 unpaired
$[Mn(H_2O)_6]^{2+}$ has maximum. Statement I is true.
Statement II: $[NiCl_4]^{2-}$: $Ni^{2+}$ ($d^8$), tetrahedral → paramagnetic
$[Ni(CO)_4]$: $Ni^0$ ($d^{10}$) → diamagnetic
$[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}$: $Ni^{2+}$ ($d^8$), square planar → diamagnetic
Only pair $\{[Ni(CO)_4], [Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}\}$ has both diamagnetic. Count = 1, not 2. Statement II is false.

q: $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}{ }^{2-}$, in acidic medium, disproportionates to :
question: $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}{ }^{2-}$, in acidic medium, disproportionates to :
solution:
$MnO_4^{2-}$ (manganate) has Mn in +6 oxidation state.
In acidic medium, it undergoes disproportionation where Mn is simultaneously oxidized and reduced:
Oxidation: $+6 \rightarrow +7$ (forms $MnO_4^-$, permanganate)
Reduction: $+6 \rightarrow +4$ (forms $MnO_2$)
Reaction: $3MnO_4^{2-} + 4H^+ \rightarrow 2MnO_4^- + MnO_2 + 2H_2O$

Consider the following reactions:
$\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{KHSO}_{4}+\mathrm{NaHSO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
In the product ' $C$ ', ' $X$ ' is the number of $O_{2}^{2-}$ units, ' $Y$ ' is the total number oxygen atoms present and ' $Z$ ' is the oxidation state of $C r$. The value of $X+Y+Z$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question:
Consider the following reactions:
$\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{KHSO}_{4}+\mathrm{NaHSO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
In the product ' $C$ ', ' $X$ ' is the number of $O_{2}^{2-}$ units, ' $Y$ ' is the total number oxygen atoms present and ' $Z$ ' is the oxidation state of $C r$. The value of $X+Y+Z$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Reaction 1: $NaCl + K_2Cr_2O_7 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow CrO_2Cl_2$ (A, chromyl chloride)
Reaction 2: $CrO_2Cl_2 + 4NaOH \rightarrow Na_2CrO_4 + 2NaCl + 2H_2O$, B = $Na_2CrO_4$
Reaction 3: $Na_2CrO_4 + H_2SO_4 + H_2O_2 \rightarrow CrO_5 + Na_2SO_4 + H_2O$
C = $CrO_5$ or $CrO(O_2)_2$ (chromium peroxide, blue)
Structure: one oxo group and two peroxide ($O_2^{2-}$) units
X = number of $O_2^{2-}$ units = 2
Y = total oxygen atoms = 5
Z = oxidation state of Cr: $O^{2-}(-2) + 2 \times O_2^{2-}(-4) = -6$, so Cr = +6
$X + Y + Z = 2 + 5 + 6 = 13$

q: The pH and conductance of a weak acid (HX) was found to be 5 and $4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S}$, respectively. The conductance was measured under standard condition using a cell where the electrode plates having a surface area of $1 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ were at a distance of 15 cm apart. The value of the limiting molar conductivity is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{S} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. (nearest integer) (Given : degree of dissociation of the weak acid $(\alpha) \ll 1$)
question: The pH and conductance of a weak acid (HX) was found to be 5 and $4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S}$, respectively. The conductance was measured under standard condition using a cell where the electrode plates having a surface area of $1 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ were at a distance of 15 cm apart. The value of the limiting molar conductivity is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{S} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. (nearest integer) (Given : degree of dissociation of the weak acid $(\alpha) \ll 1$)
solution:
Given: pH = 5, so $[H^+] = 10^{-5}$ M; G = $4 \times 10^{-5}$ S; A = 1 cm²; l = 15 cm
Cell constant = l/A = 15 cm⁻¹
Specific conductivity: $\kappa = G \times (l/A) = 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 15 = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ S/cm
For weak acid HX: $[H^+] = C\alpha = 10^{-5}$ M
Using $\kappa = \frac{C\alpha \times \Lambda°_m}{1000}$
$\Lambda°_m = \frac{1000 \times \kappa}{C\alpha} = \frac{1000 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-5}} = 6 \times 10^4$ S cm² mol⁻¹
Converting: $\Lambda°_m = 6 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-4} = 6$ S m² mol⁻¹

subject: Chemistry
q: In Carius method, 0.75 g of an organic compound gave 1.2 g of barium sulphate, find percentage of sulphur (molar mass $32 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$). Molar mass of barium sulphate is $233 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
question: In Carius method, 0.75 g of an organic compound gave 1.2 g of barium sulphate, find percentage of sulphur (molar mass $32 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$). Molar mass of barium sulphate is $233 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
solution:
In Carius method, sulfur is converted to $BaSO_4$. Each mole of $BaSO_4$ contains 1 mole of S.
Mass of S = $\frac{32}{233} \times 1.2 = 0.1648$ g
Percentage of S = $\frac{0.1648}{0.75} \times 100 = 21.97\%$

q:
Identify correct statements from the following :
A. Propanal and propanone are functional isomers.
B. Ethoxyethane and methoxypropane are metamers.
C. But-2-ene shows optical isomerism.
D. But-1-ene and but-2-ene are functional isomers.
E. Pentane and 2, 2-dimethyl propane are chain isomers.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Identify correct statements from the following :
A. Propanal and propanone are functional isomers.
B. Ethoxyethane and methoxypropane are metamers.
C. But-2-ene shows optical isomerism.
D. But-1-ene and but-2-ene are functional isomers.
E. Pentane and 2, 2-dimethyl propane are chain isomers.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
A. Propanal ($CH_3CH_2CHO$) and propanone ($CH_3COCH_3$): Same formula $C_3H_6O$, different functional groups (aldehyde vs ketone) → Functional isomers. True
B. Ethoxyethane ($C_2H_5OC_2H_5$) and methoxypropane ($CH_3OC_3H_7$): Same ether group, different alkyl groups → Metamers. True
C. But-2-ene ($CH_3CH=CHCH_3$): No chiral carbon, shows geometrical isomerism (cis-trans), not optical. False
D. But-1-ene and but-2-ene: Both are alkenes, differ in position of double bond → Position isomers, not functional isomers. False
E. Pentane and 2,2-dimethylpropane: Both $C_5H_{12}$, different carbon skeleton → Chain isomers. True
Correct: A, B and E only.

subject: Chemistry
q: From the following, the least stable structure is:
question: From the following, the least stable structure is:
solution:
To determine the stability of resonating structures, we follow these rules:
1. Structures with more covalent bonds are generally more stable.
2. Structures where all atoms have complete octets are more stable.
3. Separation of opposite charges decreases stability, while like charges on adjacent atoms highly destabilize the structure.
4. Negative charge on a more electronegative atom and positive charge on a more electropositive atom is preferred.
In structure (4), there are two positive charges ($+$) located on adjacent atoms (the carbon atom and the nitrogen atom of the nitro group).
Electrostatic repulsion between like charges on adjacent atoms makes the structure highly unstable.
In structures (1), (2), and (3), like charges are not on adjacent atoms.
Therefore, structure (4) is the least stable.

($\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of ' $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=5 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$). The molar masses of elements P and Q (in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$) respectively, are :
question: Elements P and Q form two types of non-volatile, non-ionizable compounds PQ and $\mathrm{PQ}_{2}$. When 1 g of PQ is dissolved in 50 g of solvent ${ }^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}, \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}$ was 1.176 K while when 1 g of $\mathrm{PQ}_{2}$ is dissolved in 50 g of solvent ${ }^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}, \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}$ was 0.689 K. ($\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of ' $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=5 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$). The molar masses of elements P and Q (in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$) respectively, are :
solution:
Using $\Delta T_b = K_b \times \frac{w}{M} \times \frac{1000}{W}$
For PQ: $1.176 = 5 \times \frac{1}{M_{PQ}} \times \frac{1000}{50} = \frac{100}{M_{PQ}}$
$M_{PQ} = \frac{100}{1.176} = 85$ g/mol
For $PQ_2$: $0.689 = \frac{100}{M_{PQ_2}}$
$M_{PQ_2} = \frac{100}{0.689} = 145$ g/mol
$P + Q = 85$ ... (i)
$P + 2Q = 145$ ... (ii)
Subtracting: $Q = 60$ g/mol
From (i): $P = 85 - 60 = 25$ g/mol

q: 80 mL of a hydrocarbon on mixing with 264 mL of oxygen in a closed U-tube undergoes complete combustion. The residual gases after cooling to 273 K occupy 224 mL. When the system is treated with KOH solution, the volume decreases to 64 mL. The formula of the hydrocarbon is :
question: 80 mL of a hydrocarbon on mixing with 264 mL of oxygen in a closed U-tube undergoes complete combustion. The residual gases after cooling to 273 K occupy 224 mL. When the system is treated with KOH solution, the volume decreases to 64 mL. The formula of the hydrocarbon is :
solution:
KOH absorbs $CO_2$. Volume of $CO_2$ produced = $224 - 64 = 160$ mL
Unreacted $O_2 = 64$ mL, so $O_2$ consumed = $264 - 64 = 200$ mL
For hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$: $\frac{CO_2}{\text{Hydrocarbon}} = \frac{160}{80} = 2 \Rightarrow x = 2$
$O_2$ consumed per mole = $x + \frac{y}{4} = \frac{200}{80} = 2.5$
$2 + \frac{y}{4} = 2.5 \Rightarrow y = 2$
Hydrocarbon is $C_2H_2$

question:

q: Which of the following graphs between pressure ' p ' versus volume ' V ' represents the maximum work done?
question: Which of the following graphs between pressure ' p ' versus volume ' V ' represents the maximum work done?
solution:
In a pressure-volume ($P-V$) graph, the magnitude of work done is represented by the area under the curve bounded by the volume axis.
Let's analyze the area under the curve for each graph:
Graph (1) represents an expansion from $V = 22.4$ L to $V = 44.8$ L. The area is the integral $\int_{22.4}^{44.8} P dV$, which covers a significant region under the curve.
Graph (2) represents a cyclic process. The net work done is the area enclosed by the loop. This area is much smaller than the total area under the expansion curve in graph (1).
Graph (3) represents an isochoric process (constant volume $V = 22.4$ L). Since $dV = 0$, the work done $W = \int P dV = 0$.
Graph (4) also represents a cyclic process. Similar to graph (2), the net work done is the area enclosed by the loop, which is smaller than the area under the expansion curve in graph (1).
Comparing all cases, the expansion process in graph (1) covers the maximum area under the $P-V$ curve, thus representing the maximum work done.

question:
Use the following data :
\(\begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \text {Substance } & \frac{\Delta_f \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}(500 \mathrm{~K})}{\mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}} & \frac{\mathrm{~S}^{\ominus}(500 \mathrm{~K})}{\mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}} \\ \hline \mathrm{AB}(\mathrm{~g}) & 32 & 222 \\ \hline \mathrm{~A}_2(\mathrm{g}) & 6 & 146 \\ \hline \mathrm{~B}_2(\mathrm{g}) & x & 280 \\ \hline \end{array}\)
One mole each of $\mathrm{A}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{B}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ are taken in a 1 L closed flask and allowed to establish the equilibrium at 500 K. $\mathrm{A}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{B}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{AB}(\mathrm{~g})$ The value of $x\left(\mathrm{in} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)$ is $\_\_\_\_$. (Nearest integer)
(Given : $\log \mathrm{K}=2.2 \quad \mathrm{R}=8.3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$)
solution:
Reaction: $A_2(g) + B_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2AB(g)$
$\Delta G° = -2.303RT \log K = -2.303 \times 8.3 \times 500 \times 2.2 = -21026$ J/mol = $-21.03$ kJ/mol

$-21.03 = (58 - x) - (500 \times 0.018)$

q: Let $f: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be a twice differentiable function such that the quadratic equation $f(x) \mathrm{m}^{2}-2 f^{\prime}(x) \mathrm{m}+f^{\prime \prime}(x)=0$ in m, has two equal roots for every $x \in \mathbf{R}$. If $f(0)=1, f^{\prime}(0)=2$, and $(\alpha, \beta)$ is the largest interval in which the function $f\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} x-x\right)$ is increasing, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Let $f: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be a twice differentiable function such that the quadratic equation $f(x) \mathrm{m}^{2}-2 f^{\prime}(x) \mathrm{m}+f^{\prime \prime}(x)=0$ in m, has two equal roots for every $x \in \mathbf{R}$. If $f(0)=1, f^{\prime}(0)=2$, and $(\alpha, \beta)$ is the largest interval in which the function $f\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} x-x\right)$ is increasing, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For equal roots in $f(x)m^2 - 2f'(x)m + f''(x) = 0$, discriminant = 0:
$[f'(x)]^2 = f(x)f''(x)$
This gives $\frac{f''(x)}{f'(x)} = \frac{f'(x)}{f(x)}$
Integrating: $f'(x) = kf(x)$, so $f(x) = Ae^{kx}$
Using $f(0) = 1$ and $f'(0) = 2$: $A = 1$, $k = 2$
$f(x) = e^{2x}$
For $g(x) = f(\ln x - x)$:
$g'(x) = 2e^{2(\ln x - x)} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{x} - 1\right)$
$g'(x) > 0$ when $\frac{1-x}{x} > 0$, i.e., $0 < x < 1$
$(\alpha, \beta) = (0, 1)$
$\alpha + \beta = 1$

q: The area of the region, inside the ellipse $x^{2}+4 y^{2}=4$ and outside the region bounded by the curves $y=|x|-1$ and $y=1-|x|$, is :
question: The area of the region, inside the ellipse $x^{2}+4 y^{2}=4$ and outside the region bounded by the curves $y=|x|-1$ and $y=1-|x|$, is :
solution:
Ellipse: $\frac{x^2}{4} + y^2 = 1$ with $a = 2$, $b = 1$. Area $= \pi ab = 2\pi$.
Curves $y = |x| - 1$ and $y = 1 - |x|$ intersect at $x = \pm 1$, forming a rhombus with vertices $(0, 1)$, $(1, 0)$, $(0, -1)$, $(-1, 0)$.
Vertices $(0, \pm 1)$ lie on ellipse; $(\pm 1, 0)$ are inside ellipse.
Area of rhombus $= \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 2 = 2$.
Required area $= 2\pi - 2 = 2(\pi - 1)$.

q: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$ be vectors such that $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}|=\sqrt{29}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$. If $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}\left(\lambda_{1}>\lambda_{2}\right)$ are the possible values of $(\vec{c}+\vec{d}) \cdot(-7 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$, then the equation $\mathrm{K}^{2} x^{2}+\left(\mathrm{K}^{2}-5 \mathrm{~K}+\lambda_{1}\right) x y+\left(3 \mathrm{~K}+\frac{\lambda_{2}}{2}\right) y^{2}-8 x+12 y+\lambda_{2}=0$ represents a circle, for K equal to :
question: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$ be vectors such that $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}|=\sqrt{29}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$. If $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}\left(\lambda_{1}>\lambda_{2}\right)$ are the possible values of $(\vec{c}+\vec{d}) \cdot(-7 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$, then the equation $\mathrm{K}^{2} x^{2}+\left(\mathrm{K}^{2}-5 \mathrm{~K}+\lambda_{1}\right) x y+\left(3 \mathrm{~K}+\frac{\lambda_{2}}{2}\right) y^{2}-8 x+12 y+\lambda_{2}=0$ represents a circle, for K equal to :
solution:
From $\vec{c} \times \vec{a} = \vec{a} \times \vec{d}$ where $\vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}$, we get $(\vec{c} + \vec{d}) \times \vec{a} = 0$.
So $\vec{c} + \vec{d} = t\vec{a}$. Given $|\vec{c} + \vec{d}| = \sqrt{29}$: $|t| \cdot \sqrt{29} = \sqrt{29}$, so $t = \pm 1$.
$(\vec{c} + \vec{d}) \cdot (-7\hat{i} + 2\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}) = \pm(-14 + 6 + 12) = \pm 4$.
$\lambda_1 = 4$, $\lambda_2 = -4$.
For circle: Coeff of $x^2$ = Coeff of $y^2$: $K^2 = 3K - 2 \Rightarrow K = 1, 2$.
Coeff of $xy = 0$: $K^2 - 5K + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow K = 1, 4$.
Common value: $K = 1$.

q: Let PQ and MN be two straight lines touching the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x-6 y-3=0$ at the points A and B respectively. Let O be the centre of the circle and $\angle \mathrm{AOB}=\pi / 3$. Then the locus of the point of intersection of the lines PQ and MN is :
question: Let PQ and MN be two straight lines touching the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x-6 y-3=0$ at the points A and B respectively. Let O be the centre of the circle and $\angle \mathrm{AOB}=\pi / 3$. Then the locus of the point of intersection of the lines PQ and MN is :
solution:
Circle: $(x-2)^2 + (y-3)^2 = 16$, center O = (2, 3), radius $r = 4$.
For tangents from external point P touching at A and B with $\angle AOB = \pi/3$:
In quadrilateral OAPB: $\angle APB = \pi - \pi/3 = 2\pi/3$, so $\angle OPA = \pi/3$.
In right triangle OAP: $\sin(\pi/3) = \frac{4}{OP}$.
$OP = \frac{8}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{8\sqrt{3}}{3}$.
Locus: $(x-2)^2 + (y-3)^2 = \frac{64}{3}$.
$3(x^2 + y^2) - 12x - 18y - 25 = 0$.

q: If $x^{2}+x+1=0$, then the value of $\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{4}+\left(x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)^{4}+\left(x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}\right)^{4}+\ldots+\left(x^{25}+\frac{1}{x^{25}}\right)^{4}$ is:
question: If $x^{2}+x+1=0$, then the value of $\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{4}+\left(x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)^{4}+\left(x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}\right)^{4}+\ldots+\left(x^{25}+\frac{1}{x^{25}}\right)^{4}$ is:
solution:
Given $x^2 + x + 1 = 0$, so $x = \omega$ (primitive cube root of unity) with $x^3 = 1$.
From the equation: $x + \frac{1}{x} = -1$.
Since $x^3 = 1$, value of $x^n + x^{-n}$ has period 3:
$n \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$: $x^n + x^{-n} = 2$
$n \equiv 1, 2 \pmod{3}$: $x^n + x^{-n} = -1$
Fourth powers: $(2)^4 = 16$, $(-1)^4 = 1$.
For $n = 1$ to $25$: 8 multiples of 3 contribute $8 \times 16 = 128$, remaining 17 terms contribute $17 \times 1 = 17$.
Total $= 128 + 17 = 145$.

q: The value of $\int_{-\pi / 6}^{\pi / 6}\left(\frac{\pi+4 x^{11}}{1-\sin (|x|+\pi / 6)}\right) d x$ is equal to:
question: The value of $\int_{-\pi / 6}^{\pi / 6}\left(\frac{\pi+4 x^{11}}{1-\sin (|x|+\pi / 6)}\right) d x$ is equal to:
solution:
Split: $\int_{-\pi/6}^{\pi/6} \frac{\pi}{1 - \sin(|x| + \pi/6)} dx + \int_{-\pi/6}^{\pi/6} \frac{4x^{11}}{1 - \sin(|x| + \pi/6)} dx$.
Second integral = 0 (odd function over symmetric interval).
First integral: $2\pi \int_{0}^{\pi/6} \frac{1}{1 - \sin(x + \pi/6)} dx$.
Let $u = x + \pi/6$: $= 2\pi \int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/3} \frac{1}{1 - \sin u} du$.
$\frac{1}{1-\sin u} = \sec^2 u + \sec u \tan u$.
$= 2\pi [\tan u + \sec u]_{\pi/6}^{\pi/3} = 2\pi[(\sqrt{3}+2) - \sqrt{3}] = 4\pi$.

q: $6 \int_{0}^{\pi}|(\sin 3 x+\sin 2 x+\sin x)| d x$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: $6 \int_{0}^{\pi}|(\sin 3 x+\sin 2 x+\sin x)| d x$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Using $\sin 3x + \sin x = 2\sin 2x \cos x$:
$\sin 3x + \sin 2x + \sin x = \sin 2x(2\cos x + 1) = 2\sin x \cos x(2\cos x + 1)$
Zeros in $[0, \pi]$: $x = 0, \pi/2, 2\pi/3, \pi$
Expression is positive on $(0, \pi/2)$, negative on $(\pi/2, 2\pi/3)$, positive on $(2\pi/3, \pi)$
Let $F(x) = -\frac{\cos 3x}{3} - \frac{\cos 2x}{2} - \cos x$
$F(0) = -\frac{11}{6}$, $F(\pi/2) = \frac{1}{2}$, $F(2\pi/3) = \frac{5}{12}$, $F(\pi) = \frac{5}{6}$
$\int_0^{\pi/2} = \frac{7}{3}$, $\left|\int_{\pi/2}^{2\pi/3}\right| = \frac{1}{12}$, $\int_{2\pi/3}^{\pi} = \frac{5}{12}$
Total $= \frac{7}{3} + \frac{1}{12} + \frac{5}{12} = \frac{17}{6}$
$6 \times \frac{17}{6} = 17$

q: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution curve of the differential equation $\left(1+x^{2}\right) \mathrm{d} y+\left(y-\tan ^{-1} x\right) d x=0, y(0)=1$. Then the value of $y(1)$ is :
question: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution curve of the differential equation $\left(1+x^{2}\right) \mathrm{d} y+\left(y-\tan ^{-1} x\right) d x=0, y(0)=1$. Then the value of $y(1)$ is :
solution:
Rewrite as $\frac{dy}{dx} + \frac{y}{1+x^2} = \frac{\tan^{-1}x}{1+x^2}$.
IF $= e^{\tan^{-1}x}$.
$y \cdot e^{\tan^{-1}x} = \int \frac{\tan^{-1}x}{1+x^2} \cdot e^{\tan^{-1}x} dx$.
Let $t = \tan^{-1}x$: $= \int te^t dt = e^t(t-1) + C$.
$y = \tan^{-1}x - 1 + Ce^{-\tan^{-1}x}$.
Using $y(0) = 1$: $C = 2$.
$y(1) = \frac{\pi}{4} - 1 + \frac{2}{e^{\pi/4}}$.

question: Let the foci of a hyperbola coincide with the foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{36}+\frac{y^{2}}{16}=1$. If the eccentricity of the hyperbola is 5, then the length of its latus rectum is :
solution:
For ellipse $\frac{x^2}{36} + \frac{y^2}{16} = 1$: $a = 6$, $b = 4$, $c = \sqrt{36-16} = 2\sqrt{5}$.
Foci of ellipse: $(\pm 2\sqrt{5}, 0)$.
For hyperbola with eccentricity $e = 5$ and foci at $(\pm 2\sqrt{5}, 0)$:
$e = \frac{C}{A} \Rightarrow A = \frac{2\sqrt{5}}{5}$.
$C^2 = A^2 + B^2 \Rightarrow 20 = \frac{4}{5} + B^2 \Rightarrow B^2 = \frac{96}{5}$.
Latus rectum $= \frac{2B^2}{A} = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{96}{5}}{\frac{2\sqrt{5}}{5}} = \frac{96}{\sqrt{5}}$.

q: If the domain of the function $f(x)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x-5}{11-3 x}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(2 x^{2}-3 x+1\right)$ is the interval $[\alpha, \beta]$, then $\alpha+2 \beta$ is equal to :
question: If the domain of the function $f(x)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x-5}{11-3 x}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(2 x^{2}-3 x+1\right)$ is the interval $[\alpha, \beta]$, then $\alpha+2 \beta$ is equal to :
solution:
For $\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{2x-5}{11-3x}\right)$: $-1 \leq \frac{2x-5}{11-3x} \leq 1$ gives $x \leq \frac{16}{5}$ or $x > 6$.
For $\sin^{-1}(2x^2 - 3x + 1)$: $-1 \leq 2x^2 - 3x + 1 \leq 1$.
Lower bound always satisfied (discriminant $< 0$).
Upper bound gives $x(2x-3) \leq 0$, so $0 \leq x \leq \frac{3}{2}$.
Intersection of both conditions: $[0, \frac{3}{2}]$.
Thus $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \frac{3}{2}$.
$\alpha + 2\beta = 0 + 3 = 3$.

q: Let $f: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow(0, \infty)$ be a twice differentiable function such that $f(3)=18, f^{\prime}(3)=0$ and $f^{\prime \prime}(3)=4$. Then $\lim _{x \rightarrow 1}\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{f(2+x)}{f(3)}\right)^{\frac{18}{(x-1)^{2}}}\right)$ is equal to :
question: Let $f: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow(0, \infty)$ be a twice differentiable function such that $f(3)=18, f^{\prime}(3)=0$ and $f^{\prime \prime}(3)=4$. Then $\lim _{x \rightarrow 1}\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{f(2+x)}{f(3)}\right)^{\frac{18}{(x-1)^{2}}}\right)$ is equal to :
solution:
Let $T = \lim_{x \to 1} \left( \dfrac{f(x+2)}{f(3)} \right)^{\dfrac{18}{(x-1)^2}}$ ; $1^{\infty}$ form
$\Rightarrow T = e^{\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{18}{(x-1)^2} \left( \dfrac{f(x+2) - f(3)}{f(3)} \right)}$
$\Rightarrow T = e^{\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{18}{(x-1)^2} \left( \dfrac{f(x+2) - f(3)}{18} \right)}$
$\Rightarrow T = e^{\lim_{x \to 1} \left( \dfrac{f(x+2) - f(3)}{(x-1)^2} \right)}$ $\dfrac{0}{0}$ form, apply L'pital
$\Rightarrow T = e^{\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{f'(x+2)}{2(x-1)}}$ ; $\dfrac{0}{0}$ form, apply L'pital
$\Rightarrow T = e^{\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{f''(x+2)}{2}}$ $= e^{\dfrac{4}{2}} = e^2$
$\Rightarrow \log_e(T) = 2$

q: For some $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbf{R}$, let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}\alpha & 2 \\ 1 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $B=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 1 & \beta\end{array}\right]$ be such that $A^{2}-4 A+2 I=B^{2}-3 B+I=O$. Then $\left(\operatorname{det}\left(\operatorname{adj}\left(A^{3}-B^{3}\right)\right)\right)^{2}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: For some $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbf{R}$, let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}\alpha & 2 \\ 1 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $B=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 1 & \beta\end{array}\right]$ be such that $A^{2}-4 A+2 I=B^{2}-3 B+I=O$. Then $\left(\operatorname{det}\left(\operatorname{adj}\left(A^{3}-B^{3}\right)\right)\right)^{2}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
From $A^2 - 4A + 2I = O$: characteristic equation is $\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 2 = 0$.
For $A$: $\alpha + 2 = 4 \Rightarrow \alpha = 2$.
From $B^2 - 3B + I = O$: characteristic equation is $\lambda^2 - 3\lambda + 1 = 0$.
For $B$: $\beta + 1 = 3 \Rightarrow \beta = 2$.
$A^3 = 14A - 8I$, $B^3 = 8B - 3I$.
$A^3 - B^3 = 14A - 8B - 5I = \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 20 \\ 6 & 7 \end{bmatrix}$.
$\det(A^3 - B^3) = 105 - 120 = -15$.
For 2×2 matrix: $\det(\text{adj}(M)) = \det(M)$.
$(\det(\text{adj}(A^3 - B^3)))^2 = 225$.

q: Let O be the vertex of the parabola $x^{2}=4 y$ and Q be any point on it. Let the locus of the point P, which divides the line segment OQ internally in the ratio $2: 3$ be the conic C. Then the equation of the chord of $C$, which is bisected at the point $(1,2)$, is :
question: Let O be the vertex of the parabola $x^{2}=4 y$ and Q be any point on it. Let the locus of the point P, which divides the line segment OQ internally in the ratio $2: 3$ be the conic C. Then the equation of the chord of $C$, which is bisected at the point $(1,2)$, is :
solution:
Parabola $x^2 = 4y$, vertex O = (0, 0), Q = $(2t, t^2)$ on parabola.
P divides OQ in ratio 2:3: $P = \left(\frac{4t}{5}, \frac{2t^2}{5}\right)$.
Eliminating $t$: $x^2 = \frac{8y}{5}$ (conic C with $a = \frac{2}{5}$).
For chord with midpoint $(h, k)$: $hx - 2ay = h^2 - 2ak$.
At $(1, 2)$: $x - \frac{4y}{5} = 1 - \frac{8}{5} = -\frac{3}{5}$.
$5x - 4y + 3 = 0$.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of strictly increasing functions $f$ from the set $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ to the set $\{1,2,3, \ldots., 9\}$ such that $f(i) \neq i$ for $1 \leq i \leq 6$, is equal to :
question: The number of strictly increasing functions $f$ from the set $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ to the set $\{1,2,3, \ldots., 9\}$ such that $f(i) \neq i$ for $1 \leq i \leq 6$, is equal to :
solution:
For strictly increasing $f$, if we choose elements $a_1 < a_2 < \cdots < a_6$, then $f(i) = a_i$.
Since $f(i) \geq i$ always, condition $f(i) \neq i$ means $f(i) \geq i + 1$ for all $i$.
Let $h(i) = f(i) - i$. Then $h(i) \geq 1$, $h(6) \leq 3$, and $h$ is non-decreasing.
So $h(i) \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ for all $i$.
Number of non-decreasing sequences of length 6 from $\{1, 2, 3\} = \binom{8}{2} = 28$.

q: Let $\mathrm{S}=\{(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n}): \mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n} \in\{1,2,3, \ldots.., 50\}\}$. If the number of elements $(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})$ in S such that $6^{\mathrm{m}}+9^{\mathrm{n}}$ is a multiple of 5 is $p$ and the number of elements ($m, n$) in $S$ such that $m+n$ is a square of a prime number is q, then $\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Let $\mathrm{S}=\{(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n}): \mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n} \in\{1,2,3, \ldots.., 50\}\}$. If the number of elements $(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})$ in S such that $6^{\mathrm{m}}+9^{\mathrm{n}}$ is a multiple of 5 is $p$ and the number of elements ($m, n$) in $S$ such that $m+n$ is a square of a prime number is q, then $\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For $6^m + 9^n \equiv 0 \pmod{5}$:
$6 \equiv 1 \pmod{5}$ and $9 \equiv -1 \pmod{5}$
So $6^m + 9^n \equiv 1 + (-1)^n \pmod{5}$
This equals 0 only when n is odd.
Number of odd n in {1,...,50} = 25, and m can be any of 50 values.
$p = 50 \times 25 = 1250$
For $m + n$ = square of prime, possible values are 4, 9, 25, 49.
$m + n = 4$: 3 pairs
$m + n = 9$: 8 pairs
$m + n = 25$: 24 pairs
$m + n = 49$: 48 pairs
$q = 3 + 8 + 24 + 48 = 83$
$p + q = 1250 + 83 = 1333$

q: The sum of all the roots of the equation $(x-1)^{2}-5|x-1|+6=0$, is :
question: The sum of all the roots of the equation $(x-1)^{2}-5|x-1|+6=0$, is :
solution:
Let $t = |x-1|$ where $t \geq 0$.
Equation becomes $t^2 - 5t + 6 = 0$.
$(t-2)(t-3) = 0$ gives $t = 2$ or $t = 3$.
For $|x-1| = 2$: $x = 3$ or $x = -1$.
For $|x-1| = 3$: $x = 4$ or $x = -2$.
Sum of roots $= 3 + (-1) + 4 + (-2) = 4$.

q: Let $a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, \ldots$ be G.P. of increasing positive terms such that $a_{2} \cdot a_{3} \cdot a_{4}=64$ and $a_{1}+a_{3}+a_{5}=\frac{813}{7}$. Then $a_{3}+a_{5}+a_{7}$ is equal to :
question: Let $a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, \ldots$ be G.P. of increasing positive terms such that $a_{2} \cdot a_{3} \cdot a_{4}=64$ and $a_{1}+a_{3}+a_{5}=\frac{813}{7}$. Then $a_{3}+a_{5}+a_{7}$ is equal to :
solution:
Let first term be $a$ and common ratio $r > 1$.
From $a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot a_4 = 64$: $(ar)(ar^2)(ar^3) = a^3r^6 = 64 \Rightarrow ar^2 = 4$.
From $a_1 + a_3 + a_5 = \frac{813}{7}$: $a(1 + r^2 + r^4) = \frac{813}{7}$.
Substituting $a = \frac{4}{r^2}$: $4\left(\frac{1}{r^2} + 1 + r^2\right) = \frac{813}{7}$.
Let $u = r^2$: $28u^2 - 785u + 28 = 0$ gives $u = 28$ (since $r > 1$).
$a_3 + a_5 + a_7 = ar^2(1 + r^2 + r^4) = 4(1 + 28 + 784) = 3252$.

q: Let $a_{1}=1$ and for $n \geqslant 1, a_{n+1}=\frac{1}{2} a_{n}+\frac{n^{2}-2 n-1}{n^{2}(n+1)^{2}}$. Then $\left|\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(a_{n}-\frac{2}{n^{2}}\right)\right|$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Let $a_{1}=1$ and for $n \geqslant 1, a_{n+1}=\frac{1}{2} a_{n}+\frac{n^{2}-2 n-1}{n^{2}(n+1)^{2}}$. Then $\left|\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(a_{n}-\frac{2}{n^{2}}\right)\right|$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Let $b_n = a_n - \frac{2}{n^2}$.
$b_{n+1} = \frac{1}{2}a_n + \frac{n^2-2n-1}{n^2(n+1)^2} - \frac{2}{(n+1)^2}$.
Simplifying: $b_{n+1} = \frac{1}{2}b_n + \frac{(n+1)^2 + n^2 - 2n - 1 - 2n^2}{n^2(n+1)^2} = \frac{1}{2}b_n$.
So $b_n$ is GP with ratio $\frac{1}{2}$.
$b_1 = 1 - 2 = -1$, so $b_n = -\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n-1}$.
$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n = -\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{2}} = -2$.
$\left|\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(a_n - \frac{2}{n^2}\right)\right| = 2$.

q: The number of relations, defined on the set $\{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}\}$, which are both reflexive and symmetric, is equal to:
question: The number of relations, defined on the set $\{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}\}$, which are both reflexive and symmetric, is equal to:
solution:
For a set with $n = 4$ elements, reflexive condition requires all diagonal pairs $(a,a), (b,b), (c,c), (d,d)$ to be included.
For symmetric relations, non-diagonal pairs come in symmetric pairs. Number of such pairs = $\binom{4}{2} = 6$.
For each pair, we either include both elements or exclude both → 2 choices each.
Total reflexive and symmetric relations = $2^6 = 64$.

q: Let the mean and variance of 7 observations $2,4,10, x, 12,14, y, x>y$, be 8 and 16 respectively. Two numbers are chosen from $\{1,2,3, x-4, y, 5\}$ one after another without replacement, then the probability, that the smaller number among the two chosen numbers is less than 4, is :
question: Let the mean and variance of 7 observations $2,4,10, x, 12,14, y, x>y$, be 8 and 16 respectively. Two numbers are chosen from $\{1,2,3, x-4, y, 5\}$ one after another without replacement, then the probability, that the smaller number among the two chosen numbers is less than 4, is :
solution:
Mean = 8 gives $x + y = 14$.
Variance = 16 gives $(x-8)^2 + (y-8)^2 = 4$.
Solving: $x = 8$, $y = 6$ (since $x > y$).
Set becomes $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 5\} = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$.
Total ways to choose 2 numbers = $\binom{6}{2} = 15$.
Favorable cases (smaller $< 4$): smaller = 1 gives 5 pairs, smaller = 2 gives 4 pairs, smaller = 3 gives 3 pairs.
Total favorable = 12.
Probability $= \frac{12}{15} = \frac{4}{5}$.

q:
Let $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ be the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $(5,4,2)$ on the line $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})$.
Then the length of the projection of the vector $\alpha \hat{i}+\beta \hat{j}+\gamma \hat{k}$ on the vector $6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ is :
question:
Let $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ be the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $(5,4,2)$ on the line $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})$.
Then the length of the projection of the vector $\alpha \hat{i}+\beta \hat{j}+\gamma \hat{k}$ on the vector $6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ is :
solution:
Line: $\vec{r} = (-1, 3, 1) + \lambda(2, 3, -1)$, Point P = (5, 4, 2).
Any point on line: $Q = (-1 + 2\lambda, 3 + 3\lambda, 1 - \lambda)$.
$\vec{PQ} = (-6 + 2\lambda, -1 + 3\lambda, -1 - \lambda)$.
For perpendicular: $\vec{PQ} \cdot (2, 3, -1) = 0$ gives $14\lambda - 14 = 0$, so $\lambda = 1$.
Foot $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) = (1, 6, 0)$.
Projection of $(1, 6, 0)$ on $(6, 2, 3)$: $\frac{|6 + 12 + 0|}{\sqrt{49}} = \frac{18}{7}$.

q: The value of $\operatorname{cosec} 10^{\circ}-\sqrt{3} \sec 10^{\circ}$ is equal to :
question: The value of $\operatorname{cosec} 10^{\circ}-\sqrt{3} \sec 10^{\circ}$ is equal to :
solution:
$\operatorname{cosec} 10° - \sqrt{3} \sec 10° = \frac{\cos 10° - \sqrt{3} \sin 10°}{\sin 10° \cos 10°}$.
Numerator: $\cos 10° - \sqrt{3} \sin 10° = 2\left(\frac{1}{2}\cos 10° - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin 10°\right) = 2\cos 70° = 2\sin 20°$.
Denominator: $\sin 10° \cos 10° = \frac{1}{2}\sin 20°$.
Result $= \frac{2\sin 20°}{\frac{1}{2}\sin 20°} = 4$.

q: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}=8 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ be vector such that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$. If $\vec{c} \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=4$, then $|\vec{a}+\vec{c}|^{2}$ is equal to :
question: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}=8 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ be vector such that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$. If $\vec{c} \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=4$, then $|\vec{a}+\vec{c}|^{2}$ is equal to :
solution:
Let $\vec{c} = x\hat{i} + y\hat{j} + z\hat{k}$.
From $\vec{a} \times \vec{c} = \vec{b}$: $z - y = 4$, $z + 2x = 7$, $y + 2x = 3$.
From $\vec{c} \cdot (\hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k}) = 4$: $x + y + z = 4$.
Solving: $y = -1$, $z = 3$, $x = 2$.
$\vec{c} = 2\hat{i} - \hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$.
$\vec{a} + \vec{c} = (-1+2)\hat{i} + (2-1)\hat{j} + (2+3)\hat{k} = \hat{i} + \hat{j} + 5\hat{k}$.
$|\vec{a} + \vec{c}|^2 = 1 + 1 + 25 = 27$.

q: If an alpha particle with energy 7.7 MeV is bombarded on a thin gold foil, the closest distance from nucleus it can reach is $\_\_\_\_$ m. (Atomic number of gold $=79$ and $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9}$ in SI units)
question: If an alpha particle with energy 7.7 MeV is bombarded on a thin gold foil, the closest distance from nucleus it can reach is $\_\_\_\_$ m. (Atomic number of gold $=79$ and $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9}$ in SI units)
solution:
At closest approach, KE converts to PE:
$KE = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \times \frac{(2e)(Ze)}{r_{min}}$
$r_{min} = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 79 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}{7.7 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$
$= \frac{9 \times 2 \times 79 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{7.7} \times 10^{3}$
$= \frac{2275.2}{7.7} \times 10^{-16} = 2.95 \times 10^{-14}$ m

q: A parallel plate capacitor has capacitance $C$, when there is vacuum within the parallel plates. A sheet having thickness $\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{\mathrm{rd}}$ of the separation between the plates and relative permittivity $K$ is introduced between the plates. The new capacitance of the system is :
question: A parallel plate capacitor has capacitance $C$, when there is vacuum within the parallel plates. A sheet having thickness $\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{\mathrm{rd}}$ of the separation between the plates and relative permittivity $K$ is introduced between the plates. The new capacitance of the system is :
solution:
Original capacitance: $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$
After inserting dielectric of thickness $\frac{d}{3}$, system becomes two capacitors in series.
Vacuum region: $C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2d/3} = \frac{3C}{2}$
Dielectric region: $C_2 = \frac{K\varepsilon_0 A}{d/3} = 3KC$
Equivalent capacitance: $\frac{1}{C_{new}} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} = \frac{2}{3C} + \frac{1}{3KC} = \frac{2K+1}{3KC}$
$C_{new} = \frac{3KC}{2K+1}$

q: The heat generated in 1 minute between points $A$ and $B$ in the given circuit, when a battery of 9 V with internal resistance of $1 \Omega$ is connected across these points is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
question: The heat generated in 1 minute between points $A$ and $B$ in the given circuit, when a battery of 9 V with internal resistance of $1 \Omega$ is connected across these points is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
solution:
Wheatstone bridge with P = 1Ω, Q = 2Ω, R = 2Ω, S = 4Ω
$\frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{S} = \frac{1}{2}$ → Bridge is balanced, no current through middle 1Ω.
$R_{AB}$: Upper (3Ω) and Lower (6Ω) in parallel = $\frac{3 \times 6}{9} = 2Ω$
Total resistance = 1 + 2 = 3Ω
Current $I = \frac{9}{3} = 3$ A
Power between A and B: $P = I^2 R_{AB} = 9 \times 2 = 18$ W
Heat in 1 min = $18 \times 60 = 1080$ J

q: A light wave described by $E=60\left[\sin \left(3 \times 10^{15}\right) t+\sin \left(12 \times 10^{15}\right) t\right]$ (in SI units) falls on a metal surface of work function 2.8 eV. The maximum kinetic energy of ejected photoelectron is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ eV. $\left(h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}\right.$. s. and $\left.e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
question: A light wave described by $E=60\left[\sin \left(3 \times 10^{15}\right) t+\sin \left(12 \times 10^{15}\right) t\right]$ (in SI units) falls on a metal surface of work function 2.8 eV. The maximum kinetic energy of ejected photoelectron is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ eV. $\left(h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}\right.$. s. and $\left.e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
solution:
Light contains two frequencies: $\omega_1 = 3 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s and $\omega_2 = 12 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s
For maximum KE, use higher frequency $\omega_2$.
Energy: $E_2 = \frac{h\omega_2}{2\pi} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 12 \times 10^{15}}{2\pi}$
$= \frac{79.2 \times 10^{-19}}{6.28} = 12.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J $= 7.9$ eV
$KE_{max} = E_2 - \phi = 7.9 - 2.8 = 5.1$ eV

q: A conducting circular loop of area $1.0 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field which varies as $B=\sin (100 t)$ Tesla. If the resistance of the loop is $100 \Omega$, then the average thermal energy dissipated in the loop in one period is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
question: A conducting circular loop of area $1.0 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field which varies as $B=\sin (100 t)$ Tesla. If the resistance of the loop is $100 \Omega$, then the average thermal energy dissipated in the loop in one period is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
solution:
Flux: $\phi = BA = \sin(100t)$ Wb
Induced EMF: $\varepsilon = -\frac{d\phi}{dt} = -100\cos(100t)$ V
Induced current: $I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R} = -\cos(100t)$ A
Power dissipated: $P = I^2R = 100\cos^2(100t)$ W
Average power: $P_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 = 50$ W
Time period: $T = \frac{2\pi}{100}$ s
Energy in one period: $E = P_{avg} \times T = 50 \times \frac{2\pi}{100} = \pi$ J

q: A 1 m long metal rod AB completes the circuit as shown in figure. The area of circuit is perpendicular to the magnetic field of 0.10 T. If the resistance of the total circuit is $2 \Omega$ then the force needed to move the rod towards right with constant speed (v) of $1.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
question: A 1 m long metal rod AB completes the circuit as shown in figure. The area of circuit is perpendicular to the magnetic field of 0.10 T. If the resistance of the total circuit is $2 \Omega$ then the force needed to move the rod towards right with constant speed (v) of $1.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
solution:
Given: $l = 1$ m, $B = 0.10$ T, $R = 2$ Ω, $v = 1.5$ m/s
Motional EMF: $\varepsilon = Blv = 0.10 \times 1 \times 1.5 = 0.15$ V
Current: $I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R} = \frac{0.15}{2} = 0.075$ A
Magnetic force on rod: $F = BIl = 0.10 \times 0.075 \times 1 = 7.5 \times 10^{-3}$ N
For constant velocity, applied force equals magnetic force.

q:
The electric field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by :
$E_{y}=69 \sin \left[0.6 \times 10^{3} x-1.8 \times 10^{11} t\right] \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$.
The expression for magnetic field associated with this electromagnetic wave is $\_\_\_\_$ T.
question:
The electric field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by :
$E_{y}=69 \sin \left[0.6 \times 10^{3} x-1.8 \times 10^{11} t\right] \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$.
The expression for magnetic field associated with this electromagnetic wave is $\_\_\_\_$ T.
solution:
Given $E_y = 69 \sin[0.6 \times 10^3 x - 1.8 \times 10^{11} t]$ V/m
Wave propagates along +x (form $kx - \omega t$), $\vec{E}$ is along y.
For EM wave: $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$ = direction of propagation
$\hat{j} \times \hat{k} = \hat{i}$, so $\vec{B}$ is along z-direction.
$B_0 = \frac{E_0}{c} = \frac{69}{3 \times 10^8} = 2.3 \times 10^{-7}$ T
E and B are in phase for plane EM wave.
$B_z = 2.3 \times 10^{-7} \sin[0.6 \times 10^3 x - 1.8 \times 10^{11} t]$ T

q: A point charge of $10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ is placed at origin. The work done in moving a point charge $2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ from point $A(4,4,2) \mathrm{m}$ to $B(2,2,1) \mathrm{m}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J. $\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{o}}=9 \times 10^{9}\right.$ in SI units $)$
question: A point charge of $10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ is placed at origin. The work done in moving a point charge $2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ from point $A(4,4,2) \mathrm{m}$ to $B(2,2,1) \mathrm{m}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J. $\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{o}}=9 \times 10^{9}\right.$ in SI units $)$
solution:
Distance from origin to A: $r_A = \sqrt{4^2 + 4^2 + 2^2} = 6$ m
Distance from origin to B: $r_B = \sqrt{2^2 + 2^2 + 1^2} = 3$ m
Work done: $W = q_2(V_B - V_A) = q_2 \times \frac{q_1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{r_B} - \frac{1}{r_A}\right)$
$= 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-8} \times \left(\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{6}\right)$
$= 18 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{1}{6} = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ J

subject: Physics
q: Initially a satellite of 100 kg is in a circular orbit of radius $1.5 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}$. This satellite can be moved to a circular orbit of radius $3 R_{E}$ by supplying $\alpha \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J}$ of energy. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$. (Take Radius of Earth $R_{E}=6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
question: Initially a satellite of 100 kg is in a circular orbit of radius $1.5 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}$. This satellite can be moved to a circular orbit of radius $3 R_{E}$ by supplying $\alpha \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J}$ of energy. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$. (Take Radius of Earth $R_{E}=6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
solution:
Total energy of satellite in orbit: $E = -\frac{GMm}{2r} = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2r}$
$E_1 = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2(1.5R_E)} = -\frac{gR_E m}{3}$
$E_2 = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2(3R_E)} = -\frac{gR_E m}{6}$
Energy supplied $= E_2 - E_1 = -\frac{gR_E m}{6} + \frac{gR_E m}{3} = \frac{gR_E m}{6}$
$= \frac{10 \times 6 \times 10^6 \times 100}{6} = 10^9$ J $= 1000 \times 10^6$ J
$\alpha = 1000$

subject: Physics
q: A 4 kg mass moves under the influence of a force $\vec{F}=\left(4 t^{3} \hat{i}-3 t \hat{j}\right) \mathrm{N}$ where $t$ is the time in second. If mass starts from origin at $t=0$, the velocity and position after $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ will be:
question: A 4 kg mass moves under the influence of a force $\vec{F}=\left(4 t^{3} \hat{i}-3 t \hat{j}\right) \mathrm{N}$ where $t$ is the time in second. If mass starts from origin at $t=0$, the velocity and position after $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ will be:
solution:
Given $\vec{F} = (4t^3\hat{i} - 3t\hat{j})$ N, m = 4 kg
$\vec{a} = \frac{\vec{F}}{m} = t^3\hat{i} - \frac{3t}{4}\hat{j}$
Integrating with $\vec{v}(0) = 0$:
$\vec{v} = \frac{t^4}{4}\hat{i} - \frac{3t^2}{8}\hat{j}$
At $t = 2$: $\vec{v} = \frac{16}{4}\hat{i} - \frac{12}{8}\hat{j} = 4\hat{i} - \frac{3}{2}\hat{j}$
Integrating with $\vec{r}(0) = 0$:
$\vec{r} = \frac{t^5}{20}\hat{i} - \frac{t^3}{8}\hat{j}$
At $t = 2$: $\vec{r} = \frac{32}{20}\hat{i} - \frac{8}{8}\hat{j} = \frac{8}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j}$

subject: Physics
q: A current carrying solenoid is placed vertically and a particle of mass $m$ with charge $Q$ is released from rest. The particle moves along the axis of solenoid. If $g$ is acceleration due to gravity then the acceleration (a) of the charged particle will satisfy :
question: A current carrying solenoid is placed vertically and a particle of mass $m$ with charge $Q$ is released from rest. The particle moves along the axis of solenoid. If $g$ is acceleration due to gravity then the acceleration (a) of the charged particle will satisfy :
solution:
Inside a solenoid, magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is uniform and parallel to the axis.
Particle moves along the axis, so velocity $\vec{v}$ is parallel to $\vec{B}$.
Magnetic force $\vec{F} = Q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) = 0$ (since $\vec{v} \parallel \vec{B}$)
Only gravity acts on the particle.
Acceleration $a = g$

q: In an experiment the values of two spring constants were measured as $k_{1}=(10 \pm 0.2) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$ and $k_{2}=(20 \pm 0.3) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$. If these springs are connected in parallel, then the percentage error in equivalent spring constant is :
question: In an experiment the values of two spring constants were measured as $k_{1}=(10 \pm 0.2) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$ and $k_{2}=(20 \pm 0.3) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$. If these springs are connected in parallel, then the percentage error in equivalent spring constant is :
solution:
For parallel combination: $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 = 10 + 20 = 30$ N/m
For sum, absolute errors add:
$\Delta k_{eq} = \Delta k_1 + \Delta k_2 = 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.5$ N/m
Percentage error $= \frac{\Delta k_{eq}}{k_{eq}} \times 100 = \frac{0.5}{30} \times 100 = 1.67\%$

q: Water flows through a horizontal tube as shown in the figure. The difference in height between the water columns in vertical tubes is 5 cm and the area of cross-sections at $A$ and $B$ are $6 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and $3 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ respectively. The rate of flow will be $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{s}$. (take $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
question: Water flows through a horizontal tube as shown in the figure. The difference in height between the water columns in vertical tubes is 5 cm and the area of cross-sections at $A$ and $B$ are $6 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and $3 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ respectively. The rate of flow will be $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{s}$. (take $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
solution:
Given: $h = 5$ cm, $A_1 = 6$ cm², $A_2 = 3$ cm², $g = 1000$ cm/s²
By continuity: $A_1v_1 = A_2v_2 \Rightarrow v_2 = 2v_1$
By Bernoulli's equation: $P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$
Since $P_1 - P_2 = \rho gh$:
$\rho gh = \frac{1}{2}\rho(4v_1^2 - v_1^2) = \frac{3\rho v_1^2}{2}$
$v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1000 \times 5}{3}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{3}}$ cm/s
Rate of flow $Q = A_1v_1 = 6 \times \frac{100}{\sqrt{3}} = 200\sqrt{3}$ cm³/s

subject: Physics
q: A collimated beam of light of diameter 2 mm is propagating along $x$-axis. The beam is required to be expanded in a collimated beam of diameter 14 mm using a system of two convex lenses. If first lens has focal length 40 mm, then the focal length of second lens is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
question: A collimated beam of light of diameter 2 mm is propagating along $x$-axis. The beam is required to be expanded in a collimated beam of diameter 14 mm using a system of two convex lenses. If first lens has focal length 40 mm, then the focal length of second lens is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
solution:
For beam expander using two convex lenses:
Magnification $= \frac{d_2}{d_1} = \frac{f_2}{f_1}$
$\frac{14}{2} = \frac{f_2}{40}$
$f_2 = 7 \times 40 = 280$ mm

q: In a microscope the objective is having focal length $f_{o}=2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and eye-piece is having focal length $f_{e}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. The tube length is 32 cm. The magnification produced by this microscope for normal adjustment is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: In a microscope the objective is having focal length $f_{o}=2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and eye-piece is having focal length $f_{e}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. The tube length is 32 cm. The magnification produced by this microscope for normal adjustment is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For microscope at normal adjustment:
$M = \frac{L}{f_o} \times \frac{D}{f_e}$
where $L = 32$ cm, $f_o = 2$ cm, $f_e = 4$ cm, $D = 25$ cm
$M = \frac{32}{2} \times \frac{25}{4} = 16 \times 6.25 = 100$

subject: Physics
q: A uniform rod of mass $m$ and length $l$ suspended by means of two identical inextensible light strings as shown in figure. Tension in one string immediately after the other string is cut, is $\_\_\_\_$. $(g$ acceleration due to gravity)
question: A uniform rod of mass $m$ and length $l$ suspended by means of two identical inextensible light strings as shown in figure. Tension in one string immediately after the other string is cut, is $\_\_\_\_$. $(g$ acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
When string at B is cut, rod rotates about A.
Torque about A: $\tau = mg \times \frac{l}{2}$
Moment of inertia about A: $I_A = \frac{ml^2}{3}$
Angular acceleration: $\alpha = \frac{mg \cdot l/2}{ml^2/3} = \frac{3g}{2l}$
Acceleration of COM: $a_{cm} = \alpha \times \frac{l}{2} = \frac{3g}{4}$
Newton's second law: $mg - T = ma_{cm}$
$T = mg - \frac{3mg}{4} = \frac{mg}{4}$

q: Two identical thin rods of mass $M \mathrm{~kg}$ and length $L \mathrm{~m}$ are connected as shown in figure. Moment of inertia of the combined rod system about an axis passing through point $P$ and perpendicular to the plane of the rods is $\frac{x}{12} \mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Two identical thin rods of mass $M \mathrm{~kg}$ and length $L \mathrm{~m}$ are connected as shown in figure. Moment of inertia of the combined rod system about an axis passing through point $P$ and perpendicular to the plane of the rods is $\frac{x}{12} \mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For vertical rod with axis at end P: $I_1 = \frac{ML^2}{3}$
For horizontal rod centered at distance L from P:
$I_2 = \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \frac{M}{L}(x^2 + L^2)dx = \frac{M}{L}\left[\frac{x^3}{3} + L^2x\right]_{-L/2}^{L/2}$
$= \frac{M}{L}\left[\frac{L^3}{12} + L^3\right] = \frac{13ML^2}{12}$
Total: $I = \frac{ML^2}{3} + \frac{13ML^2}{12} = \frac{4ML^2 + 13ML^2}{12} = \frac{17ML^2}{12}$
$x = 17$

subject: Physics
q: The given circuit works as :
question: The given circuit works as :
solution:
Let the inputs be A and B. The circuit is composed of NOR gates.
A NOR gate with its inputs tied together acts as a NOT gate.
The output of the first NOR gate (input A) is $Y_1 = \overline{A+A} = \bar{A}$.
The output of the second NOR gate (input B) is $Y_2 = \overline{B+B} = \bar{B}$.
These outputs are fed into a third NOR gate. Its output is $Y_3 = \overline{Y_1 + Y_2} = \overline{\bar{A} + \bar{B}}$.
Using De Morgan's theorem, $Y_3 = (\overline{\bar{A}}) \cdot (\overline{\bar{B}}) = A \cdot B$.
This output $Y_3$ is the input to the fourth NOR gate, which is configured as a NOT gate.
The final output is $Y_{out} = \overline{Y_3} = \overline{A \cdot B}$.
The expression $\overline{A \cdot B}$ represents a NAND gate.

q: An aluminium and steel rods having same lengths and cross-sections are joined to make total length of 120 cm at $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The coefficient of linear expansion of aluminium and steel are $24 \times 10^{-6} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1.2 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, respectively. The length of this composite rod when its temperature is raised to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: An aluminium and steel rods having same lengths and cross-sections are joined to make total length of 120 cm at $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The coefficient of linear expansion of aluminium and steel are $24 \times 10^{-6} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1.2 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, respectively. The length of this composite rod when its temperature is raised to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
Total length at 30°C = 120 cm, each rod = 60 cm
$\alpha_{Al} = 24 \times 10^{-6}$/°C, $\alpha_{Steel} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$/°C
$\Delta T = 100 - 30 = 70$°C
$\Delta L_{Al} = 60 \times 24 \times 10^{-6} \times 70 = 0.1008$ cm
$\Delta L_{Steel} = 60 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 70 = 0.0504$ cm
Total expansion $= 0.1008 + 0.0504 = 0.1512$ cm
Final length $= 120 + 0.1512 = 120.15$ cm

q:
A gas based geyser heats water flowing at the rate of 5.0 litres per minute from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $87^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The rate of consumption of the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{s}$.
(Take heat of combustion of gas $=5.0 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{g}$) specific heat capacity of water $=4200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} \cdot{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
question:
A gas based geyser heats water flowing at the rate of 5.0 litres per minute from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $87^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The rate of consumption of the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{s}$.
(Take heat of combustion of gas $=5.0 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{g}$) specific heat capacity of water $=4200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} \cdot{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
solution:
Flow rate = 5.0 L/min = 5.0 kg/min, $\Delta T = 87 - 27 = 60$°C
Heat required per minute: $Q = mc\Delta T = 5.0 \times 4200 \times 60 = 1.26 \times 10^6$ J/min
If m grams of gas consumed per minute:
$m \times 5.0 \times 10^4 = 1.26 \times 10^6$
$m = \frac{1.26 \times 10^6}{5.0 \times 10^4} = 25.2$ g/min
Rate $= \frac{25.2}{60} = 0.42$ g/s

q: 10 mole of oxygen is heated at constant volume from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The change in the internal energy of the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ cal. (The molecular specific heat of oxygen at constant pressure, $C_{P}=7 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.)
question: 10 mole of oxygen is heated at constant volume from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The change in the internal energy of the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ cal. (The molecular specific heat of oxygen at constant pressure, $C_{P}=7 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.)
solution:
Given: $n = 10$ mol,
$\Delta T = 40 - 30 = 10°C$,
$C_P = 7$ cal/mol·°C,
$R = 2$ cal/mol·°C
For ideal gas: $C_V = C_P - R = 7 - 2 = 5$ cal/mol·°C
Change in internal energy: $\Delta U = nC_V\Delta T = 10 \times 5 \times 10 = 500$ cal

subject: Physics
q:
Consider a modified Bernoulli equation.
$\left(\mathrm{P}+\frac{A}{B t^{2}}\right)+\rho g(h+B t)+\frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2}=$ constant
If $t$ has the dimension of time then the dimensions of $A$ and $B$ are $\_\_\_\_$, $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
question:
Consider a modified Bernoulli equation.
$\left(\mathrm{P}+\frac{A}{B t^{2}}\right)+\rho g(h+B t)+\frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2}=$ constant
If $t$ has the dimension of time then the dimensions of $A$ and $B$ are $\_\_\_\_$, $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
solution:
All terms must have dimension of pressure $[ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$.
For $(h + Bt)$: Since $\rho gh$ has dimension of pressure, $[h] = [L]$.
For dimensional consistency: $[Bt] = [L]$, so $[B] = [LT^{-1}] = [M^0LT^{-1}]$
For $\frac{A}{Bt^2}$: $\frac{[A]}{[B][T^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$
$[A] = [B][T^2][ML^{-1}T^{-2}] = [LT^{-1}][T^2][ML^{-1}T^{-2}] = [ML^0T^{-1}]$
$[A] = [ML^0T^{-1}]$ and $[B] = [M^0LT^{-1}]$

subject: Physics
q: In a double slit experiment the distance between the slits is 0.1 cm and the screen is placed at 50 cm from the slits plane. When one slit is covered with a transparent sheet having thickness $t$ and refractive index $n(=1.5)$, the central fringe shifts by 0.2 cm. The value of $t$ is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: In a double slit experiment the distance between the slits is 0.1 cm and the screen is placed at 50 cm from the slits plane. When one slit is covered with a transparent sheet having thickness $t$ and refractive index $n(=1.5)$, the central fringe shifts by 0.2 cm. The value of $t$ is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
Given: $d = 0.1$ cm, $D = 50$ cm, $n = 1.5$, Shift $= 0.2$ cm
Optical path difference by sheet: $\Delta = (n-1)t$
Shift of central fringe: Shift $= \frac{(n-1)t \times D}{d}$
$0.2 = \frac{(1.5-1) \times t \times 50}{0.1} = \frac{0.5 \times 50 \times t}{0.1} = 250t$
$t = \frac{0.2}{250} = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ cm

subject: Physics
q: Two strings $(A, B)$ having linear densities $\mu_{A}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ and, $\mu_{B}=4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ and lengths $L_{A}=2.5 \mathrm{~m}$ and $L_{B}=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$ respectively are joined. Free ends of $A$ and $B$ are tied to two rigid supports $C$ and $D$, respectively creating a tension of 500 N in the wire. Two identical pulses, sent from $C$ and $D$ ends, take time $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$, respectively, to reach the joint. The ratio $t_{1} / t_{2}$ is :
question: Two strings $(A, B)$ having linear densities $\mu_{A}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ and, $\mu_{B}=4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ and lengths $L_{A}=2.5 \mathrm{~m}$ and $L_{B}=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$ respectively are joined. Free ends of $A$ and $B$ are tied to two rigid supports $C$ and $D$, respectively creating a tension of 500 N in the wire. Two identical pulses, sent from $C$ and $D$ ends, take time $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$, respectively, to reach the joint. The ratio $t_{1} / t_{2}$ is :
solution:
Wave velocity in string: $v = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$
Time to reach joint: $t = \frac{L}{v} = L\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{T}}$
$t_1 = L_A\sqrt{\frac{\mu_A}{T}}$ and $t_2 = L_B\sqrt{\frac{\mu_B}{T}}$
$\frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{L_A}{L_B} \times \sqrt{\frac{\mu_A}{\mu_B}} = \frac{2.5}{1.5} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 10^{-4}}}$
$= \frac{5}{3} \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{5}{3\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5\sqrt{2}}{6} \approx 1.18$

subject: Physics
q: Potential energy ($V$) versus distance ($x$) is given by the graph. Rank various regions as per the magnitudes of the force (F) acting on a particle from high to low.
question: Potential energy ($V$) versus distance ($x$) is given by the graph. Rank various regions as per the magnitudes of the force (F) acting on a particle from high to low.
solution:
Force is related to potential energy by $F = -\frac{dV}{dx}$.
Magnitude of force $|F| = \left|\frac{dV}{dx}\right|$ = |slope of V-x graph|
From graph coordinates: A(1, 0), B(1.5, 1), C(2, 4), D(4, 4), E(5, 3)
$|F_{AB}| = \frac{1-0}{1.5-1} = 2$
$|F_{BC}| = \frac{4-1}{2-1.5} = 6$
$|F_{CD}| = \frac{4-4}{4-2} = 0$
$|F_{DE}| = \frac{|3-4|}{5-4} = 1$
Ranking: $F_{BC} > F_{AB} > F_{DE} > F_{CD}$
